card.title: The Circle of Fifths
card.teaser: One diagram unlocks almost every song ever written. Why does this circle work like magic?
card.coverImagePrompt: A luminous circular diagram of musical notes glowing in gold against a deep navy background, concentric rings, candlelight texture, like a mystical astrolabe crossed with sheet music

lessons.title: The cheat code of music
lessons.body: Ask a jazz pianist, a pop songwriter, and a Baroque composer what they all secretly study, and the answer is the same: a single circle of twelve letters. The Circle of Fifths tells you which chords sound good together, which keys are related, and why a song modulates up at the bridge and you suddenly feel it in your chest.

visual.imagePrompt: A pianist's hands hovering over a softly lit keyboard with a translucent Circle of Fifths floating above, ethereal, moody

lessons.title: What a 'fifth' actually is
lessons.body: Start on any note — say C. Count up seven semitones (white and black keys together) and you land on G. That's a perfect fifth. Do it again from G and you get D. Keep going and after twelve jumps you arrive back at C. That loop is the Circle of Fifths.

keyTerms.definition: The interval of seven semitones — one of the most consonant sounds in music
The set of sharps or flats that define the home key of a piece

visual.imagePrompt: Close-up of a piano keyboard with C and G keys glowing, arrows arcing between them to show the interval

lessons.title: Why it sounds so good
lessons.body: A perfect fifth is the simplest frequency ratio in music after the octave — exactly 3:2. Our ears love these ratios; they feel stable. Stacking fifths also happens to generate every note in the Western scale, which is why moving around the circle always feels logical rather than jarring. Neighboring keys on the circle share almost all the same notes, so transitions sound smooth.

visual.imagePrompt: Two overlapping sine waves at a 3:2 frequency ratio, glowing cyan and magenta, elegant physics illustration

lessons.title: Hidden in your favorite songs
lessons.body: 'Let It Be,' 'Don't Stop Believin',' and roughly half of pop music runs on a four-chord loop borrowed straight from adjacent points on the circle. Even Bach's 'Well-Tempered Clavier' was a victory lap through all twenty-four keys of the circle — a demo reel showing off what the diagram could do.
lessons.funFact: The circle was formalized by Ukrainian composer Nikolai Diletskii in 1679 in a treatise called Grammatika — long before it reached Western textbooks.

visual.imagePrompt: Vintage vinyl records fanned out on a wooden table with a hand-drawn Circle of Fifths on parchment beside them

lessons.title: A map for your ear
lessons.body: Once you see the circle, you can't un-see it. A key change in a song isn't random — it's usually a neighbor on the circle. The tension in a chord progression often comes from jumping across the circle. It's a map of musical gravity, and every composer is just navigating it.

quiz.question: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
quiz.explanation: A perfect fifth spans seven semitones — the interval from C to G on a piano.

quiz.question: What frequency ratio defines a perfect fifth?
quiz.explanation: A perfect fifth is a 3:2 ratio — one of the simplest and most consonant intervals in music.

quiz.question: Why do neighboring keys on the circle sound smooth together?
options: They share almost all the same notes
They are always in a minor key
quiz.explanation: Adjacent keys on the Circle of Fifths differ by only one sharp or flat, so their notes overlap almost entirely.
